mainEntity.name: I didn\'t tell anyone when I slipped on oil in the warehouse last week. Is my claim dead now?

acceptedAnswer.text: No, but it\'s at risk. If your employer had "actual knowledge" of the incident or if you report it now, you may still be able to win the claim, though the insurance company will likely fight harder.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Missing a reporting deadline is a common tactic insurance companies use to "shut the door" on a claim, but it\'s not always the end. In Utah, if you can show your employer had "actual knowledge" of the injury (e.g., they saw you fall, or you told a lead hand), the 30-day notice requirement can sometimes be waived. In Idaho, if the employer wasn\'t "prejudiced" by the delay, you might still have a path forward.

However, the longer you wait, the more likely the insurer will argue your injury happened "off the clock."

How to revive a late claim:
- Find witnesses who saw the accident or heard you complain about pain that day.
- Show "actual knowledge" by the company (did they bring you an ice pack?).
- Get a medical opinion that the injury is consistent with the warehouse event you described.

Be honest about why you waited (e.g., "I thought it was just a strain and didn\'t want to cause trouble"). This is a very human reason that judges sometimes understand.

A lawyer is essential here to argue the legal exceptions that can save a "late" claim from being dismissed.
